audiophileNote: Elliot Scheiner's live MTV mix opens with twelve-string acoustic and conga rim-shots before any vocal. The conga strikes between 0:35 and 0:55 demonstrate transient attack with no compression. The audience halo wrapping the sides establishes a believable hall depth, and Don Felder's nylon-string entrance at 1:15 reveals upper-mid timbre. Stereophile R2D4 perennial.

track: Speak to Me / Breathe (In the Air)
album: The Dark Side of the Moon
audiophileNote: Alan Parsons' Abbey Road tape master. The opening heartbeat, alarm-clock chatter, and screams float across discrete depth planes before Mason's drum fill at 1:14 collapses everything into the band. The classic test for whether a system can place individual sound effects in three-dimensional space.

Jim Anderson recorded direct-to-DSD at Chicago Recording. Michael Arnopol's upright bass walks at 0:30 with audible string-against-fingerboard slap; Mark Walker's brushed snare dances between channels. A Modern Cool was Stereophile's Recording of the Month and lives on TAS Super Disc lists.

Bray Studios live capture. The acoustic guitar body resonance and Clapton's intimate vocal sit forward; the small studio audience reverb tail decays naturally for almost two seconds after each phrase. Russ Titelman's mix is a benchmark for nylon-string and cedar-top timbre.

Phill Brown engineered to two-inch analog tape with the studio darkened. The eight-second silence before the guitar feedback at 4:00 is its own listening test — system noise floor, room tone, and the transition from near-zero to wall-of-sound demonstrate macro-dynamic range as well as any rock recording.

Recorded at Sunset Sound with Biff Dawes. Waits' broken-glass vocal sits dead center while marimba, clarinet, and pump organ occupy distinct lateral positions. The percussion section uses real industrial objects — listen for separation between metallic clanks at 1:40.

Val Valentin's Verve session at Capitol Studios. Webster's tenor breath and key-pad clicks are audible; the room sound around Ray Brown's bass at 1:50 reveals exactly how far back he sat from the mic. AAA Analogue Productions reissue is reference.

track: Prelude (Cello Suite No. 1 in G major, BWV 1007)
album: The Cello Suites: Inspired by Bach
audiophileNote: Steven Epstein recorded Ma's 1712 Davidov Stradivarius at Skywalker Sound. Bow noise on string and the cello body's internal resonance are intact; the hall's two-second decay surrounds every long note. A staple of Sony's audiophile catalog.

Chesky Records' minimal-mic JVC XRCD master. Pidgeon's voice locks center, marimba sits at 10 o'clock, bass at 4 o'clock. One of the most-played demo tracks at high-end audio shows for two decades because the placement is exact and the noise floor near-inaudible.

Joe Marciano's Capitol Studios sessions. David Piltch's upright bass sounds physically large between 0:00 and 0:40 — pluck, body resonance, and string decay are individually audible. The Cisco Music 180g LP is reference; CD master is also excellent.

Roscoe Beck's analog production with Bernie Becker mastering. The album-length test record of the late 80s; the orchestral pads on this track open wide and sustain across the soundstage while Warnes' voice remains intimately forward. 20th-anniversary remaster is the keeper.

David Kershenbaum's spare production. Chapman's voice has visible chest resonance; the acoustic guitar's wound-string detail and the bass guitar's pick attack are unmasked because the arrangement is so sparse. A Mobile Fidelity gold CD candidate.

Roger Nichols and Elliot Scheiner spent six months mixing Gaucho. Bernard Purdie's signature shuffle hi-hat at 0:30 sits high-right; the Rhodes and synth bass occupy distinct timbral lanes that lesser systems smear. Cindy Mizelle's backing vocals reveal layering depth.

Recorded at Right Track New York with one Neumann pair on the bass and ribbon mics on the guitar. Haden's bass body fills the lower octaves with audible wood; Metheny's nylon-string sits forward-left in a perceptibly rectangular room. ECM-grade reverb tail.

Phil Ramone engineered at A&R Studios on three-track tape. Getz's tenor sits hard right, Gilberto's guitar and quiet vocal hard left, Astrud Gilberto enters dead center at 1:36. The Verve/Acoustic Sounds 45rpm reissue lifts a veil that's been on every CD pressing.

Rudy Van Gelder mono session at Hackensack. Milt Jackson's vibraphone mallets have explosive transient attack; the entire quintet locks into discrete spaces despite single-channel mixdown. Analogue Productions mono SACD is the reference master.

Claus Ogerman's strings, recorded by Phil Ramone at A&R New York. Jobim's piano is bone-dry and intimate while the orchestra sits in a deep arc behind him — a perfect demonstration of front-to-back layering on a single recording.

album: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band
audiophileNote: Geoff Emerick's four-track masterpiece. The 41-piece orchestral crescendo at 1:45 and 3:50 covers more than 30 dB of dynamic range; the final E-major chord decays for over 40 seconds. 2017 Giles Martin remix unmasked details buried for fifty years.

Neil Davidge's bass synth at 0:00 sits below 40 Hz and stays there for 90 seconds, building tension. Horace Andy's vocal floats high over compressed drum breaks; the explosion at 4:30 swaps the sub for a wall of distorted guitar. Bass extension test.

track: Pass the Hatchet, I Think I'm Goodkind
album: I Am Not Afraid of You and I Will Beat Your Ass
audiophileNote: Roger Moutenot tracked the band live at Nashville's House of David. The eleven-minute krautrock workout maintains separation between two saturated guitars, organ drone, and Hubley's tom-heavy kit even at peak density. Drum mics breathe with the room.

Robin Guthrie's wall of chorused guitars stacks at least six distinct layers from forward-mid to far-back. Liz Fraser's vocal multitracks blur into airy harmony clouds. Recorded at September Sound; 4AD's master reveals far more depth than the original CD pressing.

Eno, Daniel Lanois, and Roger Eno layered DX7 pads in Grant Avenue Studio. Notes hold for 20+ seconds with audible chorus modulation; the soundstage extends well beyond the speakers/headphone shells with no transient cues to anchor it. A noise-floor test.

William Bevan composed entirely in SoundForge with no quantization. Vinyl-crackle texture, pitched-up R&B vocal samples, and sub-bass below 30 Hz coexist with field-recorded rain in distinct depth planes. Reveals every dropout in lossy codecs.

Recorded on a Yamaha Disklavier reproducing piano with no overdubs. Hammer-against-string transients, sustain pedal noise, and the room's natural decay are exposed by the spare arrangement. Two minutes of pure piano timbre as a reference test.

Tom Dowd recorded at Atlantic's NYC studio on three-track tape. Coltrane's tenor breath and reed buzz sit forward and slightly right; Tommy Flanagan's piano occupies clear left placement. Rhino's 2018 Mono and Stereo box exposes the original tape head bumps.

Teo Macero engineered at Columbia 30th Street Studio — a converted church with a 60-foot ceiling. The room's natural reverb cradles Booker Ervin's tenor while Mingus's bass anchors. Mobile Fidelity's One-Step UltraDisc has become the definitive master.

Rudy Van Gelder's Englewood Cliffs studio. Tony Williams's ride cymbal sizzle, Ron Carter's audibly woody bass, and Freddie Hubbard's cornet attack share a tight quartet space. Music Matters 33 RPM reissue is the reference.

track: I Believe (When I Fall in Love It Will Be Forever)
audiophileNote: Bob Margouleff and Malcolm Cecil's TONTO synth runs the bass line; Wonder layered Rhodes, harmonica, drums, and lead/backing vocals himself. Mobile Fidelity's gold CD lets you trace each overdub. A masterclass in how dense overdubs can still breathe.

Roger Nichols' first all-digital production, recorded on Sony PCM-3324. The opening shaker, claves, and Rhodes occupy textbook stereo positions; Marcus Miller's bass has crystalline pitch definition. The flagship audiophile demo of the early CD era.

Yoshimura's two-instrument home studio on a Fender Rhodes and Yamaha CP-30. Glassy Rhodes notes hang in genuine silence — the recording's absence of noise reveals system noise floor. Light in the Attic's 2017 reissue from the original 1/4-inch tape.

Jerry Boys recorded the kora solo at Livingston Studios. Each of the 21 strings has individual transient definition; the gourd resonator's body tone is so palpable you can map its dimensions. Released by World Circuit, Boys' usual reference imprint.

album: In the Heart of the Moon
audiophileNote: Recorded over three days at the Hotel Mandé in Bamako with a portable rig — Touré's guitar hard left, Diabaté's kora hard right, room ambience between them. Produced by Nick Gold; Grammy winner. Stripped-down mic technique reveals exact spatial placement.

Geoff Emerick on Studio Two's 8-track. McCartney's bass walks center-low with audible string buzz; Ringo's hand-claps and rim taps are panned hard right. Giles Martin's 2019 Atmos remix gave new clarity, but the original Mobile Fidelity LP is still the audiophile pick.

Teo Macero's three-channel Columbia 30th Street session. Joe Morello's drum solo from 2:35 to 3:55 is one of the cleanest snare and tom recordings ever captured — each rim shot has explosive attack and zero compression. Analogue Productions 45rpm reissue is reference.

Bob Johnston produced at Columbia Studio E in Nashville. Cohen's voice has audible dryness and lip noise; the picked acoustic guitar's wound-string buzz and the breathy female chorus reveal microphone technique. MFSL Original Master Recording is the reference.

Howie B and Nellee Hooper layered orchestral strings, programmed beats, and Björk's vocal in a Bahamian villa. The breakbeat at 2:00 enters with a 12 dB step; the strings underneath occupy a deeper plane than the synthetic elements. Test of macro-dynamic linearity.

Tico Records original; Puente's timbales lock dead center with audible head tension and stick wood. The conga, bongo, and timbale ensemble between 0:35 and 1:30 demands separation across three closely related percussion timbres. Vintage RVG-school session.

Bruce Botnick recorded inside the Doors' Workshop with Manzarek's Rhodes piano on the right. The opening rain field-recording extends physically beyond the headphone enclosure; Morrison's whispered vocal sits behind his sung vocal in a deliberate depth split.

Will Thornbury's mono live recording at the Sunset School auditorium in Carmel. Garner's piano left-hand has volcanic transient attack; the audience hush surrounding each rubato pause creates palpable hall air. 2015 Complete Concert remaster.

Bob Brewer engineered at Hitsville USA. James Jamerson's bass guitar walks unmistakably across the lower octaves; Gaye's lead vocal sits center while his own ad-libbed responses pan in from both sides. The original tape is still the audiophile target.

Larry Klein produced and Geoff Emerick engineered the 71-piece London Symphony Orchestra plus Wayne Shorter and Herbie Hancock. Mitchell's smoke-aged voice holds against full string crescendos. A masterclass in vocal-versus-orchestra balance.

album: It Might as Well Be Swing
audiophileNote: Sonny Burke produced with Quincy Jones arranging Basie's band. Recorded at United Recording. Sinatra's vocal phrasing dynamics across the chorus, the brass stab punctuations, and the rhythm section's swing all sit in clean, separated lanes. Mobile Fidelity SACD is reference.

track: Symphony No. 3 in C minor — IV. Adagio
audiophileNote: Lewis Layton's 3-mic Living Stereo session at Symphony Hall. The pipe organ's 32-foot pedal at the finale demands subwoofer-grade extension; Berj Zamkochian's organ entries can shake your chest with the soundstage extending across an actual concert hall.

track: Rite of Spring — Part I: Adoration of the Earth
audiophileNote: Recorded at Henry Wood Hall by Signum Classics. The Augurs of Spring at 3:00 layers stamping strings against irregular brass attacks; separation between sections is the chief test. Modern digital capture with classical-grade dynamic range — quiet woodwind passages sit near the noise floor.

track: Concerto for Two Violins in D minor, BWV 1043 — Largo
artist: J.S. Bach / Arthur Grumiaux / Herman Krebbers
audiophileNote: Philips analog session at La Chaux-de-Fonds. Grumiaux's violin sits left, Krebbers right, the chamber strings behind them in clear depth tiers. The interweaving violin lines remain individually trackable — a reference for two-instrument imaging.

artist: Samuel Barber / Leonard Slatkin / St. Louis Symphony
audiophileNote: Telarc digital recording at Powell Hall by Jack Renner using a Soundstream 50kHz system. The eight-minute crescendo from pianissimo to fortissimo is one of the most-cited examples of micro-dynamic shading. Hall reverb extends three full seconds after the final chord.

Solo piano captured at La Sauna Recording Studio in Varese. Einaudi's Steinway hammer attacks and pedal noise are unmasked; long sustains decay against a near-silent floor. A cleaner piano-only reference than most major-label classical releases.

Manfred Eicher's ECM New Series production. Solo violin and piano in a vast, hand-built reverb space. Notes hang for ten seconds; the silences between them are part of the composition. Reveals every dither artifact and noise-floor flaw.

Joe Boyd produced and John Wood engineered at Sound Techniques London. Harry Robinson's string arrangement sits behind Drake's intimately mic'd voice and acoustic guitar. The 2013 Tuck Box pressing from original tapes is the audiophile choice.

John Simon produced at Columbia Studio E, NYC. The fingerpicked nylon-string guitar opens with audible string-against-fingerboard movement; Nancy Priddy's harmony vocal sits a clear half-step deeper than Cohen's lead. Mobile Fidelity OMR remaster.

The 80 Hz sub-bass wobble at 1:00 demands genuine LF extension or it disappears entirely; Blake's voice sits in painful intimacy above it. Mixed by Mike Marsh. The contrast between near-silence and chest-thumping bass is a classic torture test for both headphones and DACs.

Justin Vernon recorded alone in a Wisconsin hunting cabin on a Shure SM57 and an old SM7. The cabin's wooden room tone, layered falsetto harmonies, and acoustic guitar body sound are the entire production. A reference for room-as-instrument capture.

Henry Lewy engineered with Mitchell at A&M Studios. Jaco Pastorius's fretless bass roams freely across the lower midrange while Mitchell's open-tuned guitar holds rhythmic ground. The bass is so prominent and distinct it functions as a second lead instrument.

Bob Ferguson produced at RCA Studio B Nashville. Parton's voice has audible chest resonance and the tiniest catch on each verse; the acoustic guitar's chiming arpeggios and Mac Magaha's fiddle sit in textbook stereo positions. AAA Analogue Productions reissue.

Otis self-produced and played every instrument at Wally Heider's Studio. Layered Wurlitzer, vibes, fuzz guitar, and proto-drum-machine inhabit distinct depth planes. Luaka Bop's 2001 reissue and Sundazed LP both restore details lost on first CDs.

Leon Ware co-produced with Gaye at Motown Hitsville West. Layered backing vocals (all Gaye), Henry Davis's bass guitar, and percussion sit in deliberate front-to-back tiers. The 2003 deluxe edition restored the original LP master tones.

T Bone Burnett produced live-to-tape at Capitol Studio B. Bareilles' piano and voice were captured in a single take with the band; her breath cues, pedal noise, and the room around her are intact. 2020 Grammy-winner for Best American Roots Performance.

Andy Johns engineered at Olympic Studio One. Bonham's drums hit with explosive transient impact; Jones's bass is more present than on most Zeppelin tracks. Page's 2014 remasters from the original tapes are the audiophile reference.

Bernard Purdie's shuffle and Chuck Rainey's bass are textbook studio-musician precision. Roger Nichols' obsessive multi-tracking lets you isolate each percussion line. The 1999 Cisco/Rhino remaster keeps Bob Ludwig's mastering intact.

Phil Ramone engineered at A&R Studios. Hubert Laws's flute, Jerome Richardson's tenor, and Roland Hanna's piano lock into discrete spatial positions; the vibraphone mallet attacks are razor-sharp. A&M's analog session — Speakers Corner LP is the reference.

David Kershenbaum produced at A&M Studios. Baez's voice has crystalline upper-mid presence; the layered acoustic guitars and Joni Mitchell-style harmonies sit in clear stereo positions. A&M's original LP and the Mobile Fidelity gold CD both shine.

Terry Brown produced at Le Studio Morin Heights. The seven-minute progressive piece moves through six distinct dynamic sections; Lifeson, Lee, and Peart each maintain individual lanes through dense polyrhythmic passages. Mobile Fidelity SACD is reference.

Eddy Offord engineered at Advision Studios London. The harmonic-rich opening guitar from Howe, Squire's lead bass, and Wakeman's organ all play simultaneously without masking. Steven Wilson's 2015 stereo remix from original multitracks is the modern reference.

album: The Famous 1938 Carnegie Hall Jazz Concert
audiophileNote: Bell Labs engineer Albert Marx's wax-cylinder capture from the second balcony. Despite the 1938 vintage, Gene Krupa's tom-tom attacks have shocking transient impact and you can hear the hall's actual depth. Sony's 1999 remaster from the original lacquers.

Arif Mardin produced at Sorcerer Sound NYC; recorded to analog tape. Jones's piano has visible damper-pedal noise; the brushed snare halo and her voice's chest tone are the entire mix. Classic Records 200g LP is the audiophile pressing.

Roger Nichols engineered. Chuck Rainey's slap bass intro is the most famous bass test in audiophile circles — pitch, slap-and-pop attack, and sustain are all individually trackable. Different track from L002's 'Aja' — same album but a distinct lesson opportunity.

Paul Northfield engineered at Le Studio. Peart's drum kit is one of the cleanest rock drum recordings — every cymbal bell, snare ghost note, and tom rim has individual transient definition. MFSL Original Master Recording on 200g vinyl is reference.

track: Cantus in Memory of Benjamin Britten
audiophileNote: ECM New Series — Manfred Eicher produced. The piece consists of a tubular bell tolling and a string descent; the recording's enormous reverb tail and slow crescendo from near-silence to fortissimo are pure dynamic test material. Released on the seminal Tabula Rasa LP.

George Martin scored the piccolo trumpet; Geoff Emerick recorded at Abbey Road. David Mason's piccolo trumpet solo has unmistakable timbre and is panned distinctly from the rhythm section. Giles Martin's 2017 Sgt. Pepper-era remix elevated the spatial separation.

Joel Little produced in his Auckland studio. The sub-808 bass kick lands at almost exactly 60 Hz; the snap snare panned hard right and Lorde's stacked vocals dead-center make a textbook minimalist mix. A modern pop reference for headphone bass extension.

Damon Albarn produced with Stephen Sedgwick mixing at Studio 13. Mick Jones's guitar, Sinfonia ViVA strings, programmed beats, and Snoop Dogg's vocal cameo all coexist with audible spatial differentiation. The 2010 LP remains the cleanest pressing.

John Leckie produced at RAK Studios. Yorke's vocal was reportedly recorded live in two takes, with Greenwood's guitar wash arriving at 2:55 in a 15 dB step. Different track from L006 (Let Down). Capitol's 2016 vinyl reissue from the original tapes is reference.

track: Famous Drum Solo (Inna Gadda Da Vida — short version)
audiophileNote: Don Casale engineered at Ultrasonic Recording Studios. Ron Bushy's two-minute drum solo at 6:35 features tom-fills moving across the entire stereo field. The Friday Music 180g LP and Audio Fidelity SACD restore the original Atco master.

Bob Clearmountain mixed at Power Station. Bernard Edwards's bass guitar is the most-sampled bass line in music history; pitch definition and pluck transient are immediate. Tony Thompson's snare and Nile Rodgers's chinkin' guitar each occupy clear lanes.

Fred Catero engineered at Wally Heider's Studio C, San Francisco. Santana's PRS through the Twin Reverb has unmistakable mid-range bloom; Jose Chepito Areas's timbales pop hard left, Mike Carabello's congas hard right. MFSL Ultradisc One-Step LP is the audiophile keeper.

Bush self-produced at Wickham Farm with the Fairlight CMI III. Nine layered vocal tracks, Morris Pert's percussion ensemble, and the Fairlight orchestral pads must all stay separate. The 2018 remaster from the original 1985 ½-inch tape is reference.

Conny Plank co-produced at Kling Klang Studio. The Synthanorma sequencer locks each percussion sound in its own panned position; the precision is so exact it's a textbook stereo imaging test. 2009 EMI remaster from the original analog tapes.

Birgir Jón Birgisson engineered in Sigur Rós's converted swimming-pool studio Sundlaugin. The slow-build drum reveal at 3:30 lifts the entire mix from whisper to full ensemble; the marching tom hits are physical. Different album from L021 (() ). 2014 vinyl reissue is reference.

Scott Litt produced at Bad Animals, Seattle. The single-take piano (Mike Mills on the John Keane piano) was recorded in one room with one stereo pair. John Paul Jones arranged the strings. The room sound is intact and the piano sits in palpable physical space.

Hugh Murphy produced at Chipping Norton Studios. Raphael Ravenscroft's sax solo at 0:42 has unmistakable reed warmth and breath texture; the rhythm section sits cleanly behind. United Artists' analog master and Audio Fidelity's SACD both restore the warmth.

Steve Albini engineered at Q Division Studios on his characteristic ambient drum technique. The drums sound enormous because the room mics are way back; quiet-loud-quiet dynamics are the song's structure. 4AD's Mobile Fidelity Original Master on vinyl is reference.

Decca recorded at Walthamstow Town Hall. Eight cellos plus soprano — listen for individual instrument differentiation across the cello choir, then for Fleming's vocal sitting precisely in front of them. The hall's three-second reverb is intact and immersive.

track: Famous Strings (Mahler Symphony No. 5 — IV. Adagietto)
audiophileNote: John McClure recorded at Manhattan Center. The 11-minute string-and-harp Adagietto contains some of the most subtle micro-dynamic gradations in the orchestral repertoire. Sony's 2014 SACD remaster from the original 3-track session tapes is reference.

Roy Thomas Baker engineered the multi-section masterpiece across six studios. The 'Galileo' choir section layers up to 180 vocal overdubs; the operatic-to-hard-rock transition at 4:08 is one of the most dramatic dynamic shifts in pop. 2011 Bob Ludwig remaster.

track: Famous Vocal (Dido's Lament — When I Am Laid in Earth)
audiophileNote: Decca recorded at Kingsway Hall, London. Baker's voice carries 30 years of operatic technique; the descending ground bass underneath is perfectly distinct. Kingsway's three-second hall reverb wraps every phrase. The Speakers Corner reissue uncovers tape detail lost on CD.

track: Famous Bass Drop (Genesis — In the Cage)
album: The Lamb Lies Down on Broadway
audiophileNote: John Burns engineered at Glaspant Manor on the band's mobile rig. The eight-minute progressive piece moves through four distinct sections; Phil Collins's drum entries, Tony Banks's mellotron pads, and Steve Hackett's tapping all maintain separation. 2008 Nick Davis remix is reference.

Verve recorded at Capitol Studio A, NYC. The Basie band's section-by-section precision — saxes, brass, rhythm — and the 'one more time' coda's brass-stab punctuations make this a benchmark for big-band capture. 2015 Acoustic Sounds 45rpm reissue is reference.

artist: The Joseph Spence and The Pinder Family
audiophileNote: Peter Siegel field-recorded a Bahamian Baptist congregation. Voices arrive from non-uniform distances — the room is real, the singers are real, the placement is unrehearsed. Nonesuch's 2008 remaster from the original master tapes preserves the field-recording immediacy.

album: Pierné: L'An Mil et Pièces d'Orgue
audiophileNote: Recorded at Saint-Sulpice on the 1862 Cavaillé-Coll organ — one of the largest pipe organs in France. The 32-foot pedal stops shake the building; reverb extends almost ten seconds. A definitive ultra-low-frequency and giant-acoustic test. BNL Productions release.

Horikawa's binaural field recordings of marbles, ping-pong balls, and household objects. Different track from L029 (Bubbles). Each impact is precisely localized in three-dimensional space; an extreme test of imaging precision. First Word Records pressing is reference.

Richard Mullen engineered at Power Station NYC. Vaughan's Stratocaster through a Fender Vibroverb has audible string scrape, finger slides, and amp breakup; the spare arrangement leaves nothing hidden. Mobile Fidelity's gold CD and 200g LP both reference-grade.

Nigel Godrich produced live-to-tape at Ocean Way Hollywood. Bossa nova rhythm, surf guitar, brass, theremin, and Beck's vocal all coexist in distinct lanes. Original DGC LP and the Mobile Fidelity SACD restore tape-warm tonal subtleties.

Aloysio de Oliveira produced at MGM Studios LA. Regina hard-left, Jobim hard-right, the smiling interplay between them perfectly imaged. Speakers Corner LP reissue is the audiophile reference. The vocal interplay sounds physically present in the room.

Riverside session at Reeves Sound Studios NYC. Different album from L017 (Waltz for Debby). LaFaro's bass walks with audible woody attack; Motian's brushed snare is perfectly differentiated from the cymbal wash. Analogue Productions 45rpm SACD is reference.

Recorded at Studio Hoche, Paris by Bernard Estardy. Barbara's piano and voice were captured in a single live take; her breath, pedal noise, and the chamber's reverb tail create one of French chanson's most audiophile-respected recordings. Mercury original LP.

Mark 'Spike' Stent mixed at Olympic Studios. Tony Kanal's bass and the Neptunes' drum programming have sharp pitch definition and transient attack; Stefani's vocal sits cleanly above. The CD master is unusually well-mastered for the era.

track: Famous Quartet (String Quartet No. 14 — II. Adagio non troppo)
audiophileNote: Da-Hong Seetoo recorded at the American Academy of Arts and Letters. The four instruments are individually placed and the cello's woody resonance, viola's nasal mid, and two violins' contrasting timbres are all distinguishable. DG Original-Image Bit Processing master.

Vee-Jay session at Universal Recording, Chicago. Hooker's foot-stomp, electric guitar, and gravel voice are recorded close-mic'd with the band's dryness intact. The Tip of the Top remaster from the original quarter-inch tape is the audiophile reference.

Geoff Emerick recorded Ringo's drums through a Leslie cabinet at Abbey Road Studio Three. Eight tape loops manipulated by all four Beatles run simultaneously; Lennon's vocal goes through a Leslie itself. Giles Martin's 2022 mix from original 4-tracks unmasks each loop.

Rudy Van Gelder recorded at Englewood Cliffs in October 1962. Blakey's drum solo at 4:30 is one of the most explosive transient passages in jazz; the band's three horns each occupy their own lateral position throughout. Analogue Productions 45rpm SACD is reference.
